title: Chemical Eye Injury
oneLiner: Ocular emergency from acid/alkali (alkali worse) causing rapid epithelial damage, corneal melt, and glaucoma; needs immediate copious irrigation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Globe perforation / penetration** — look for shallow anterior chamber, irregular pupil, Seidel test positive (leak of aqueous on fluorescein). Must rule out before any manipulation.
- **Alkali penetration into anterior chamber** — alkali (esp. ammonia, lye) saponifies lipids and rapidly penetrates cornea → raise IOP → ischaemia → irreversible vision loss. Single sign: corneal opacity with raised IOP (>25 mmHg) and conjunctival blanching (limbal ischaemia).
- **Corneal melt / perforation** — severe alkali injury may melt stroma in 30–60 minutes; look for thinning, Descemetocele, or flat anterior chamber.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute severe pain, photophobia, blepharospasm, blurred vision, foreign body sensation (may be less with alkali due to nerve damage)
- **Key exam findings:** 
  - Reduced visual acuity (VA)
  - Lid erythema/oedema, conjunctival injection, chemosis
  - Corneal epithelial defect (stains with fluorescein)
  - Limbal ischaemia (white patches at corneal limbus) — poor prognostic sign
  - Raised intraocular pressure (palpation or tonometry)
  - In severe cases: corneal opacification, anterior chamber reaction (cells/flare)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Corneal abrasion** — no chemical exposure, clear conjunctiva, normal pH
- **Ocular foreign body** — lateralised pain, fluorescein may show linear abrasion from FB; need eversion and slit-lamp
- **Conjunctivitis** — no epithelial defect, normal pH, less pain
- **Acid vs alkali injury:** 
  - Acid (e.g. H₂SO₄): causes protein coagulation → clouding that limits penetration; usually less severe
  - Alkali (e.g. ammonia, NaOH): liquefactive necrosis, penetrates deep → worse prognosis, more limbal ischaemia
- **Splitter:** **pH measurement** — acid <7, alkali >7.5; if pH >11, assume alkali injury and aggressive irrigation.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 
  - Immediate pH testing (use litmus paper or pH strips from eye station) — do before and after irrigation
  - Fluorescein staining + cobalt blue light or slit-lamp (to assess epithelial defect)
  - Seidel test (for globe perforation if suspicious)
  - Tonometry (elevated IOP common)
- **Bloods:** not routinely needed unless systemic absorption suspected (rare with alkali ingestion)
- **Imaging:** orbital CT only if globe penetration suspected (e.g., high-velocity injury)
- **Special tests:** slit-lamp examination by ophthalmology – grade severity (Roper-Hall classification)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** 
  - **Roper-Hall classification** (grades I–IV): based on corneal opacity and limbal ischaemia extent
    - Grade I: clear cornea, no ischaemia
    - Grade IV: opaque cornea, >50% limbal ischaemia
  - **Never-miss test:** **pH measurement** — do it before irrigation (if available) and continue to check after irrigation until pH 7.0–7.4.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Remove all contact lenses, particulate matter (evert eyelids, sweep fornices with cotton-tipped applicator)
  - **Copious irrigation** — use 1–2 litres of normal saline or water via IV giving set or Morgan lens. Minimum 15–30 minutes. Continue until pH normalises (7.0–7.4). Check pH every 5 minutes during irrigation.
  - Topical anaesthetic (proxymetacaine 0.5% one drop) to facilitate irrigation and exam
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral — all chemical injuries need slit-lamp evaluation within 1 hour
  - Do **not** give topical steroids until epithelial defect assessed (risk of fungal keratitis if defect present)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** (for severe injuries requiring admission)
  - Continue topical cycloplegics (e.g., cyclopentolate 1% TDS) to reduce pain and prevent synechiae
  - Topical antibiotics (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3% QDS) to prevent secondary infection
  - Topical corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% QDS) — only after corneal epithelium healed (usually day 3–5) to reduce inflammation; but avoid in first days if epithelial defect present (can impair re-epithelialisation)
  - Consider topical ascorbate (10% solutions) and citrate (10%) to reduce corneal melt (discuss with ophthalmology)
  - Oral tetracyclines (e.g., doxycycline 100mg BD) may reduce collagenase activity — [check local policy]
  - Lubrication with preservative-free artificial tears hourly
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Bandage contact lens for persistent epithelial defect
  - Amniotic membrane transplant for limbal ischaemia (day 7–14)
  - Long-term anti-glaucoma therapy if IOP raised
  - Corneal transplantation / keratoprosthesis for scarring
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: none specific; encourage oral intake
  - VTE prophylaxis: apply standard (enoxaparin if mobilising poorly)
  - Diet: NBM only if scheduled for surgery
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly for first 4 hours, then 4-hourly if stable
  - Eye chart: measure VA daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Topical anaesthetic (for exam only):** 
  - Proxymetacaine 0.5% eye drops — one drop immediately before irrigation/examination (no long-term use)
- **Cycloplegics:** 
  - Cyclopentolate 1% eye drops — one drop TDS (for pain and to break synechiae)
- **Topical antibiotics:** 
  - Ofloxacin 0.3% eye drops — one drop QDS (or chloramphenicol 0.5% if mild; check local policy)
- **Topical steroids (only after epithelial closure):**
  - Prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops — one drop QDS (taper over weeks; avoid if epithelial defect present)
- **Oral tetracycline:**
  - Doxycycline 100mg PO BD for 2–4 weeks — [check local policy] in severe alkali injury
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Steroids with corneal epithelial defect — risk of fungal/amoebic keratitis
  - Aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin) — avoid until epithelial defect heals (may be toxic to epithelium)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** not needed for topical agents
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using topical steroid (e.g., prednisolone) before confirming epithelial closure** — can cause sterile corneal melt and superinfection. Always check fluorescein staining first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal epithelial sloughing, stromal oedema, corneal perforation
  - Acute glaucoma (from inflammation or angle closure)
  - Sterile hypopyon (if severe inflammation)
  - Secondary infection (bacterial, fungal)
- **Chronic:**
  - Limbal stem cell deficiency → persistent epithelial defect, conjunctivalisation
  - Symblepharon (adhesions between conjunctiva and cornea)
  - Glaucoma (chronic angle closure)
  - Cataract (posterior subcapsular from steroids or inflammation)
  - Corneal scarring and opacification (vision loss)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality virtually zero; visual prognosis depends on initial grade (Roper-Hall). Grade I–II: good recovery with prompt irrigation. Grade III–IV: high risk of permanent vision loss (blindness in 30–50% if limbal ischaemia >50%). Requires months of topical therapy and staged surgery.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure and document pH before and after irrigation** — this is medico-legal and guides duration of irrigation.
- **Evert eyelids and sweep fornices** to remove particulate matter (especially in lime/cement injury) — retained particles cause ongoing chemical burn.
- **Continue irrigation until pH remains 7.0–7.4 for 10 minutes** after stopping — don’t stop just because pH first normalises (rebound alkalinity).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not stop irrigation early** — many believe a few litres is enough; alkali burns need at least 15–30 minutes of continuous flow.
- **Do not give topical morphine or drops with preservatives that sting** — use preservative-free preparations for long-term use.
- **Do not delay ophthalmology referral** — even “mild” alkali injury can progress; all chemical eye injuries need slit-lamp assessment within 1–2 hours.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Pain worsens, vision decreases, photophobia increases, or any purulent discharge.
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology clinic next day for repeat pH check, slit-lamp, and IOP measurement. If epithelial defect present, daily until healed. Provide an emergency contact number.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ophthalmology reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - pH >11 initially
  - Limbal ischaemia >25%
  - IOP >30 mmHg refractory to topical therapy
  - Corneal perforation suspected (Seidel positive)
  - Any deterioration in VA (drop >2 lines Snellen)
- **Crash team / ICU criteria:** rare — only if globe perforation with orbital haemorrhage or intracranial extension (CT needed). NEWS2 >5 or GCS <13 warrants anaesthetic review.

rawMarkdown:
## Chemical Eye Injury
**One-liner:** Ocular emergency from acid/alkali (alkali worse) causing rapid epithelial damage, corneal melt, and glaucoma; needs immediate copious irrigation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Globe perforation / penetration** — look for shallow anterior chamber, irregular pupil, Seidel test positive (leak of aqueous on fluorescein). Must rule out before any manipulation.
- **Alkali penetration into anterior chamber** — alkali (esp. ammonia, lye) saponifies lipids and rapidly penetrates cornea → raise IOP → ischaemia → irreversible vision loss. Single sign: corneal opacity with raised IOP (>25 mmHg) and conjunctival blanching (limbal ischaemia).
- **Corneal melt / perforation** — severe alkali injury may melt stroma in 30–60 minutes; look for thinning, Descemetocele, or flat anterior chamber.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute severe pain, photophobia, blepharospasm, blurred vision, foreign body sensation (may be less with alkali due to nerve damage)
- **Key exam findings:** 
  - Reduced visual acuity (VA)
  - Lid erythema/oedema, conjunctival injection, chemosis
  - Corneal epithelial defect (stains with fluorescein)
  - Limbal ischaemia (white patches at corneal limbus) — poor prognostic sign
  - Raised intraocular pressure (palpation or tonometry)
  - In severe cases: corneal opacification, anterior chamber reaction (cells/flare)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Corneal abrasion** — no chemical exposure, clear conjunctiva, normal pH
- **Ocular foreign body** — lateralised pain, fluorescein may show linear abrasion from FB; need eversion and slit-lamp
- **Conjunctivitis** — no epithelial defect, normal pH, less pain
- **Acid vs alkali injury:** 
  - Acid (e.g. H₂SO₄): causes protein coagulation → clouding that limits penetration; usually less severe
  - Alkali (e.g. ammonia, NaOH): liquefactive necrosis, penetrates deep → worse prognosis, more limbal ischaemia
- **Splitter:** **pH measurement** — acid <7, alkali >7.5; if pH >11, assume alkali injury and aggressive irrigation.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 
  - Immediate pH testing (use litmus paper or pH strips from eye station) — do before and after irrigation
  - Fluorescein staining + cobalt blue light or slit-lamp (to assess epithelial defect)
  - Seidel test (for globe perforation if suspicious)
  - Tonometry (elevated IOP common)
- **Bloods:** not routinely needed unless systemic absorption suspected (rare with alkali ingestion)
- **Imaging:** orbital CT only if globe penetration suspected (e.g., high-velocity injury)
- **Special tests:** slit-lamp examination by ophthalmology – grade severity (Roper-Hall classification)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** 
  - **Roper-Hall classification** (grades I–IV): based on corneal opacity and limbal ischaemia extent
    - Grade I: clear cornea, no ischaemia
    - Grade IV: opaque cornea, >50% limbal ischaemia
  - **Never-miss test:** **pH measurement** — do it before irrigation (if available) and continue to check after irrigation until pH 7.0–7.4.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Remove all contact lenses, particulate matter (evert eyelids, sweep fornices with cotton-tipped applicator)
  - **Copious irrigation** — use 1–2 litres of normal saline or water via IV giving set or Morgan lens. Minimum 15–30 minutes. Continue until pH normalises (7.0–7.4). Check pH every 5 minutes during irrigation.
  - Topical anaesthetic (proxymetacaine 0.5% one drop) to facilitate irrigation and exam
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral — all chemical injuries need slit-lamp evaluation within 1 hour
  - Do **not** give topical steroids until epithelial defect assessed (risk of fungal keratitis if defect present)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** (for severe injuries requiring admission)
  - Continue topical cycloplegics (e.g., cyclopentolate 1% TDS) to reduce pain and prevent synechiae
  - Topical antibiotics (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3% QDS) to prevent secondary infection
  - Topical corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% QDS) — only after corneal epithelium healed (usually day 3–5) to reduce inflammation; but avoid in first days if epithelial defect present (can impair re-epithelialisation)
  - Consider topical ascorbate (10% solutions) and citrate (10%) to reduce corneal melt (discuss with ophthalmology)
  - Oral tetracyclines (e.g., doxycycline 100mg BD) may reduce collagenase activity — [check local policy]
  - Lubrication with preservative-free artificial tears hourly
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Bandage contact lens for persistent epithelial defect
  - Amniotic membrane transplant for limbal ischaemia (day 7–14)
  - Long-term anti-glaucoma therapy if IOP raised
  - Corneal transplantation / keratoprosthesis for scarring
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: none specific; encourage oral intake
  - VTE prophylaxis: apply standard (enoxaparin if mobilising poorly)
  - Diet: NBM only if scheduled for surgery
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly for first 4 hours, then 4-hourly if stable
  - Eye chart: measure VA daily

### Drugs & Doses
- **Topical anaesthetic (for exam only):** 
  - Proxymetacaine 0.5% eye drops — one drop immediately before irrigation/examination (no long-term use)
- **Cycloplegics:** 
  - Cyclopentolate 1% eye drops — one drop TDS (for pain and to break synechiae)
- **Topical antibiotics:** 
  - Ofloxacin 0.3% eye drops — one drop QDS (or chloramphenicol 0.5% if mild; check local policy)
- **Topical steroids (only after epithelial closure):**
  - Prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops — one drop QDS (taper over weeks; avoid if epithelial defect present)
- **Oral tetracycline:**
  - Doxycycline 100mg PO BD for 2–4 weeks — [check local policy] in severe alkali injury
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Steroids with corneal epithelial defect — risk of fungal/amoebic keratitis
  - Aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin) — avoid until epithelial defect heals (may be toxic to epithelium)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** not needed for topical agents
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using topical steroid (e.g., prednisolone) before confirming epithelial closure** — can cause sterile corneal melt and superinfection. Always check fluorescein staining first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal epithelial sloughing, stromal oedema, corneal perforation
  - Acute glaucoma (from inflammation or angle closure)
  - Sterile hypopyon (if severe inflammation)
  - Secondary infection (bacterial, fungal)
- **Chronic:**
  - Limbal stem cell deficiency → persistent epithelial defect, conjunctivalisation
  - Symblepharon (adhesions between conjunctiva and cornea)
  - Glaucoma (chronic angle closure)
  - Cataract (posterior subcapsular from steroids or inflammation)
  - Corneal scarring and opacification (vision loss)

### Prognosis
- Mortality virtually zero; visual prognosis depends on initial grade (Roper-Hall). Grade I–II: good recovery with prompt irrigation. Grade III–IV: high risk of permanent vision loss (blindness in 30–50% if limbal ischaemia >50%). Requires months of topical therapy and staged surgery.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure and document pH before and after irrigation** — this is medico-legal and guides duration of irrigation.
- **Evert eyelids and sweep fornices** to remove particulate matter (especially in lime/cement injury) — retained particles cause ongoing chemical burn.
- **Continue irrigation until pH remains 7.0–7.4 for 10 minutes** after stopping — don’t stop just because pH first normalises (rebound alkalinity).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not stop irrigation early** — many believe a few litres is enough; alkali burns need at least 15–30 minutes of continuous flow.
- **Do not give topical morphine or drops with preservatives that sting** — use preservative-free preparations for long-term use.
- **Do not delay ophthalmology referral** — even “mild” alkali injury can progress; all chemical eye injuries need slit-lamp assessment within 1–2 hours.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Pain worsens, vision decreases, photophobia increases, or any purulent discharge.
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology clinic next day for repeat pH check, slit-lamp, and IOP measurement. If epithelial defect present, daily until healed. Provide an emergency contact number.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ophthalmology reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - pH >11 initially
  - Limbal ischaemia >25%
  - IOP >30 mmHg refractory to topical therapy
  - Corneal perforation suspected (Seidel positive)
  - Any deterioration in VA (drop >2 lines Snellen)
- **Crash team / ICU criteria:** rare — only if globe perforation with orbital haemorrhage or intracranial extension (CT needed). NEWS2 >5 or GCS <13 warrants anaesthetic review.
